results.possibleIdentity: Robert E. Myer (also known as Bob Myer) was a prominent electrical engineer and inventor at Bell Telephone Laboratories (Bell Labs) who was active from the late 1960s through the 1990s. Resident in Denville, New Jersey, he served as a Member of Technical Staff at Bell Labs for approximately 19 years, where he specialized in transistor circuit design. His early technical career involved developing fail-safe and radar tracking systems for U.S. military missile projects, including the Nike Zeus, Nike Hercules, Safeguard, and Sentinel. Myer is most famously recognized in the music technology world for his collaboration with Mike Matthews of Electro-Harmonix; he designed the legendary Big Muff Pi distortion pedal, the LPB-1 Linear Power Booster (the company's first product), the Queen Wah, the Frequency Analyzer, and the Bad Stone phase shifter. He is also credited with discovering the feedback/regeneration effect in phase shifters. Beyond commercial electronics, Myer engaged in projects at the intersection of art and technology—a hallmark of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement—including building one of the earliest transistorized music synthesizers, designing a direct-view Kirlian Aura device, and creating LED art. He graduated Magna Cum Laude with a B.S. in Electrical Engineering from the Newark College of Engineering in 1967. Over his career, he authored more than 70 patents, including a 1968 patent for a Class B transistor power amplifier and a significant feed-forward amplifier used in cellular communications. His honors include the Rudolf Kompfner Trophy and the AT&T Patent Award.
